verses.text: 'Behold my servant... mine elect, in whom my soul delighteth'; he brings forth justice gently, for 'a bruised reed shall he not break.' Matthew sees Christ here. The Servant of the LORD is meek and tender, not crushing the weak but reviving them.

'Behold my servant... mine elect, in whom my soul delighteth'; he brings forth justice gently, for 'a bruised reed shall he not break.' Matthew sees Christ here. The Servant of the LORD is meek and tender, not crushing the weak but reviving them.

'Behold my servant... mine elect, in whom my soul delighteth'; he brings forth justice gently, for 'a bruised reed shall he not break.' Matthew sees Christ here. The Servant of the LORD is meek and tender, not crushing the weak but reviving them.

The God who made the heavens calls His Servant 'for a covenant of the people, for a light of the Gentiles,' to open blind eyes and free prisoners. His mission is salvation. Christ comes to give sight and liberty to a captive world.

'I am the LORD: that is my name: and my glory will I not give to another,' and He declares new things before they spring forth. He guards His glory. The God who foretells the future shares His honour with no idol.

'I am the LORD: that is my name: and my glory will I not give to another,' and He declares new things before they spring forth. He guards His glory. The God who foretells the future shares His honour with no idol.
All the earth, the isles, and the wilderness are called to 'sing unto the LORD a new song.' Salvation summons praise. The works of God draw worship from the ends of the earth.

The LORD goes forth as a mighty man of war, yet also leads the blind 'by a way that they knew not,' making darkness light. Power and gentleness meet. He fights for His people and guides them tenderly home.

Yet His own servant is deaf and blind, seeing many things but observing not. Israel fails to perceive. The privileged can be the most spiritually dull, beholding truth without heeding it.

Though the LORD is well pleased to magnify His law, His people are robbed and spoiled, for 'they would not walk in his ways.' Judgment teaches what mercy could not. Affliction follows a people who refuse to obey.
